step.title: Step 44
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> SPACE

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 9 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 183, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Game over when SPACE splits red block incorrectly - Need to approach red blocks from correct side - For right red: approached from left, SPACE shrunk it, LEFT split it, SPACE cleared it - For left red: approached from right, SPACE expanded it (wrong!) - Need to approach left red from left side
- Goal model: Clear left red first (from left), then right red
- Plan: UPx4, LEFTx6, SPACE, RIGHT, SPACE, DOWNx2, RIGHTx4, SPACE, LEFT, SPACE
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 123 actions; a strong score needs about 58 or fewer. You are 2.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After UPx4, LEFTx4, SPACE:
- Red: boundary=[[16, 8], [16, 19], [19, 19], [20, 20], [20, 23], [23, 23], [23, 4], [20, 4], [20, 7], [19, 8]]
- Blue: (24-27, 28-39)
The red block has a complex shape now. Let me parse the boundary:
- (16, 8) -> (16, 19) -> (19, 19) -> (20, 20) -> (20, 23) -> (23, 23) -> (23, 4) -> (20, 4) -> (20, 7) -> (19, 8)
This looks like an L-shaped or irregular block. The red block expanded to include more area.
The blue is at (24-27, 28-39).
The right red is gone (it was at 24-27, 28-39 before, but now the blue is there).
Wait, the blue is at (24-27, 28-39), which is where the right red was. So the blue moved to the right red's position when I pressed SPACE.
And the left red expanded to include a larger area.
Let me think about what happened:
- Before SPACE: blue (16-19, 12-31), left red (16-19, 8-19)
- After SPACE: red has complex shape, blue (24-27, 28-39)
The blue jumped to the right red's position, and the left red expanded.
This is confusing. Let me try to understand the mechanic better.
When I press SPACE with the blue overlapping a red block:
- If the blue is to the right of the red (approaching from right), the red expands and the blue jumps to the other red block's position.
- If the blue is to the left of the red (approaching from left), the red shrinks and the blue moves to the red block's position.
Wait, that doesn't match what I observed earlier. Let me re-examine.
Earlier, for the right red:
- Blue (24-27, 20-39), right red (24-27, 28-39)
- SPACE: blue (24-27, 28-39), right red (24-27, 20-27)
So the blue moved to the right red's position, and the right red shrunk to the left of the blue's original position.
For the left red (first attempt):
- Blue (16-19, 12-31), left red (16-19, 8-11)
- SPACE: left red (16-19, 8-31), blue (24-27, 24-35)
So the left red expanded to cover the blue's position, and the blue jumped to a new position.
For the left red (second attempt):
- Blue (16-19, 12-31), left red (16-19, 8-19)
- SPACE: left red expanded to complex shape, blue (24-27, 28-39)
So the left red expanded, and the blue jumped to the right red's position.
The pattern seems to be:
- When blue overlaps with right red (blue to the left), SPACE causes the red to shrink to the left, and the blue takes the red's position.
- When blue overlaps with left red (blue to the right), SPACE causes the red to expand to the right, and the blue jumps to the other red block's position.
This suggests that the direction of the blue's approach matters. For the right red, the blue approaches from the left, and the red shrinks. For the left red, the blue approaches from the right, and the red expands.
Maybe I need to approach the left red from the left side (blue to the left of the red) to make it shrink.
From the initial state:
- Blue: (36-39, 20-39)
- Left red: (16-19, 8-19)
To approach the left red from the left:
- UPx4: blue (20-23, 20-39) -> (16-19, 20-39)
- LEFTx12: blue (16-19, 8-27) -> but this would overlap with the left red at cols 8-19
Actually, to approach from the left, the blue needs to be at cols < 8. But the blue is 20 cols wide, so it can't fit to the left of the left red without overlapping.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to clear the right red first, then deal with the left red.
Actually, looking at the current state:
- Red: complex shape including (16-19, 8-19) and (20-23, 4-23)
- Blue: (24-27, 28-39)
The red block seems to have merged or expanded. Let me try to continue from here.
Maybe I should try RIGHT, SPACE to see if the red block splits and disappears.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model:
- SPACE with blue overlapping left red caused it to expand, not shrink
- Red now has complex L-shape, blue at (24-27, 28-39)
- Right red position now occupied by blue
Goal model: Try to clear the expanded red block
Plan: Try RIGHT, SPACE to split and clear the red block

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: red complex shape, blue (24-27, 28-39)
# Try RIGHT, SPACE
result = action(['RIGHT', 'SPACE'])
print("After RIGHT, SPACE:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['b', 'R']:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: boundary={n['boundary']}")
print("  Level completed:", result.get('level_completed', False))
print("  Game over:", result.get('game_over', False))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
